coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effect of 2D covalent organic frameworks confined 0D carbon quantum dots film: Toward molecularly imprinted cathodic photoelectrochemical platform for detection of tetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mengge
Dong, Xiuxiu
Ji, Guanya
Wang, Jing
Wang, Tao
Liu, Qian
Niu, Qijian
coredata.dc:description: The development of high photoactive cathode materials combined with the formation of a stable interface are considered important factors for the selective and sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) detection of tetracycline (TC). Along these lines, in this work, a novel type II heterostructure composed of two-dimensional (2D) covalent organic frameworks confined to zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (CDs/COFs) film was successfully synthesized using the rapid in-situ polymerization method at room temperature. The PEC signal of CDs/COFs was significantly amplified by improving the light absorption and electron transfer capabilities. Furthermore, a cathodic molecularly imprinted PEC sensor (MIP-PEC) for the detection of TC was constructed through fast in-situ Ultraviolet (UV) photopolymerization on the electrode. Finally, a “turn-off” PEC cathodic signal was achieved based on the selective recognition of the imprinted cavity and the mechanism of steric hindrance increase. Under optimal conditions, the proposed sensor demonstrated a wide linear relationship with TC in the concentration range of 5.00 × 10−12–1.00 × 10−5 M, with a detection limit as low as 6.00 × 10−13 M. Meanwhile, excellent stability, selectivity, reproducibility, and applicability in real river samples was recorded. Our work provides an effective and rapid in situ construction method for fabricating highly photoactive cathode heterojunctions and uniform stable selective MIP-PEC sensing interfaces, yielding accurate antibiotics detection in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic frameworks
Carbon quantum dots
Cathodic photoelectrochemical sensor
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Detection of tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In situ synthesis of CDs/COFs on ITO
Synthesis and characterizations of the CDs/COFs
PEC performance and enhancement mechanism of CDs/COFs
Characterization and detection feasibility of the MIP-PEC sensor
Performance and recognition mechanism of MIP
Selectivity, reproducibility, reusability and stability of MIP-PEC sensor
Application of real water sample detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62101216, 62201230, 32301723), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220546), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671359, 2022T150275, 2020M671367), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD-2023-87), and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (SJCX24_2424).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CDs (A). HR-TEM image of CDs/COFs (B). Lattice fringes of CDs/COFs (C). SEM top view of ITO (D), COFs/ITO (E) and CDs/COFs/ITO (F). SEM cross section of ITO (G), COFs/ITO (H) and CDs/COFs/ITO (I). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the COFs and CDs/COFs (J). Flourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) spectrum of ITO, COFs, and CDs/COFs (K). X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) patterns of the ITO, COFs and CDs/COFs (L).

Photocurrent signals of COFs and CDs/COFs (A). UV–vis DRS spectrum of COFs (curve a) and CDs/COFs (curve b) (B). FL spectrum for COFs (curve a) and CDs/COFs (curve b) (C). IPCE of COFs and CDs/COFs (D). Electron-hole lifetime of COFs and CDs/COFs (E) and schematic illustration of the electron-transfer process (F).

Photocurrent responses (A) of CDs/COFs/ITO (curve a), MIP/CDs/COFs/ITO (curve b), rMIP/CDs/COFs/ITO (curve c) and rMIP/CDs/COFs/ITO after being immersed in 10 nM TC (curve d), 100 nM TC (curve e). EIS Nyquist plots (B) of ITO, CDs/COFs/ITO (curve a), NIP/CDs/COFs/ITO (curve b), rMIP/CDs/COFs/ITO (curve c) and rMIP/CDs/COFs/ITO immersion in 10 nM TC (curve d), 100 nM TC (curve e).

CV response of the MIP-PEC sensor (RSD = 4%) (A), photocurrent responses values of MIP-PEC sensors and NIP-PEC sensors immersed in 10 μM TC (RSD = 5%) (B). Linear fitting of Langmuir-Freundlich adsorption model (C). The FT-IR spectra of the MIP-PEC sensor rebinding to TC with different concentrations (0 nM, 10 nM, 100 nM and 1 μM) (D) and the binding mechanism of MIP and TC (E).

Photocurrent responses of the MIP-PEC sensor to different TC concentrations (from a to n) (A). Derivation of the linear calibration curve for TC quantification (n = 3) (B). Selectivity (C), interference immunity (D), reproducibility (E), 14-day stability of MIP-PEC sensor (F).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure of CDs (A), synthesis of CDs/COFs (B), the construction process, and the detection mechanism of the MIP-PEC system (C).

head.ce:title: Synergistic effect of 2D covalent organic frameworks confined 0D carbon quantum dots film: Toward molecularly imprinted cathodic photoelectrochemical platform for detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Agriculture Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Agriculture Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212013, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high photoactive cathode materials combined with the formation of a stable interface are considered important factors for the selective and sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) detection of tetracycline (TC). Along these lines, in this work, a novel type II heterostructure composed of two-dimensional (2D) covalent organic frameworks confined to zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (CDs/COFs) film was successfully synthesized using the rapid in-situ polymerization method at room temperature. The PEC signal of CDs/COFs was significantly amplified by improving the light absorption and electron transfer capabilities. Furthermore, a cathodic molecularly imprinted PEC sensor (MIP-PEC) for the detection of TC was constructed through fast in-situ Ultraviolet (UV) photopolymerization on the electrode. Finally, a “turn-off” PEC cathodic signal was achieved based on the selective recognition of the imprinted cavity and the mechanism of steric hindrance increase. Under optimal conditions, the proposed sensor demonstrated a wide linear relationship with TC in the concentration range of 5.00 × 10−12–1.00 × 10−5 M, with a detection limit as low as 6.00 × 10−13 M. Meanwhile, excellent stability, selectivity, reproducibility, and applicability in real river samples was recorded. Our work provides an effective and rapid in situ construction method for fabricating highly photoactive cathode heterojunctions and uniform stable selective MIP-PEC sensing interfaces, yielding accurate antibiotics detection in the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), as a low-cost and broad-spectrum antibiotic, is in high demand and widely used in medicine, aquaculture, and livestock farming (Qing et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2023). However, the improper and excessive use of TC inevitably leads to a high accumulation in food chains, soils, and natural water systems through various pathways. The TC residues have become a worrying environmental problem, which gives rise to the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria and resistance genes, posing long-term negative impacts on human health (Reichert et al., 2019; Kang et al., 2024; Balakrishnan et al., 2023). Therefore, it is of paramount importance to develop sensitive and selective TC detection methods for accurately assessing environmental safety and safeguarding human health (Pulicharla et al., 2017).

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors, as an advancing sensing technology, have been widely used in environmental monitoring and food analysis due to their high sensitivity, low background, low cost, handy operation, and easy miniaturization (Jia et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022; Dashtian et al., 2024). By designing highly photoactive materials and combining them with specific recognition elements, some novel PEC sensing methods have been developed in the literature for the sensitive and selective detection of TC. In terms of recognition elements, they are mainly divided into non-recognition elements (Si et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2024), aptamers (Wang et al., 2022a; Guo et al., 2022; Jiang et al., 2024) and MIP (Zhou et al., 2023; Sheng et al., 2022). Although both aptamer and MIP have good selectivity, MIP has relatively better chemical stability and low cost (Wang et al., 2022b; Liu et al., 2023a; Dashtian et al., 2022). In the fabrication process of sensors, photoelectrically active materials play a crucial role (Zhou and Tang, 2020). To solve the challenges related to the rapid recombination of electrons and holes in most photoactive materials, many heterojunctions are widely constructed to extend the carrier lifetime (Jiang et al., 2017), such as gC3N4/BiVO4 (Zhao et al., 2023), ZnO-CdS@Au (Zhang et al., 2017), CdTe-BiOBr (Liu et al., 2023b), ZIF-8/ZIS (Chen et al., 2022) and PCN-224/CDs (Wang et al., 2024). Among them, the cathode photocurrent has stronger anti-interference than the anode photocurrent (Zhang et al., 2021). Meanwhile, compared to conventional semiconductor heterojunctions, the MOFs-based heterojunctions exhibit some advantages in PEC analysis due to the large surface area, high porosity, adjustable pore size, and easy functionalization (Dashtian et al., 2021a). The spatial confinement effect is a powerful approach to improve the sensing performance (Jia et al., 2023a, 2023b). Our group encapsulated CDs within MOFs to prevent CDs leaching and improve composite stability. This encapsulation significantly enhanced the cathodic photocurrent response, increasing the PEC sensitivity and precision of the sensors. The formation of a heterojunction in the pore of MOFs is more conducive to enhancing the electron transport efficiency in the bulk phase (Zuo et al., 2024). Nevertheless, the preparation process of the MOFs-based heterojunction and the electrode structure are often relatively time-consuming, and the well-known MOFs suffer from poor thermal stability and water tolerance. Therefore, it is necessary to further develop integrated stable PEC sensing heterojunctions and interfaces to improve the efficiency and performance of PEC sensing.

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), as a new type of crystalline organic semiconductor material (Ma et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2019a; Jia et al., 2024), have attracted increasing attention, in terms of developing PEC sensors, due to their comparable advantages and better stability than MOFs (Yazdani et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2022a). The structure of 2D COFs with covalent bonding and planar ordered columnar π-conjugation ensures high chemical stability and remarkable optoelectronic properties (Wang et al., 2022c). Meanwhile, the cathodic PEC activity of COFs can effectively avoid photocorrosion and coexisting interference (Zhang et al., 2021). Among them, the D-TA COFs can in-situ grow on the electrode to construct a stable nanoscale thickness and ordered homogeneous electrode interface for achieving excellent PEC sensing capabilities. However, its application remains to be further studied in the PEC sensor for TC detection. Meanwhile, D-TA COFs still have inherent problems related to the relatively high exciton binding energy (Kashani et al., 2024). Similar to MOFs-based heterojunctions, some COFs-based heterojunctions, such as AuNPs@WO3@TpPa-1-COFs (Liu et al., 2024), F-COFs/TiO2 NTA (Wang et al., 2021a), MOFs@COFs (Xu et al., 2021), CsPbBr3@COF-V (Xiao et al., 2022), and Cu2O@TAPB-Tp-COFs (Huo et al., 2023) have been successfully developed to improve the PEC performance of pure COFs. Among them, the combination with quantum dots has attracted wide attention from the scientific community. Compared with metal-containing quantum dots, CDs have been widely used in the PEC field due to their low toxicity, environmental protection, low cost, and simple preparation procedure (Li et al., 2024a). For example, some works in the literature have developed CD@N3-COFs (Ma et al., 2022b), CDs-based CCOF-1 and CCOF-2 (Chen et al., 2020) to adjust the bridge structure at the atomic level to promote the electron transport and improve the photoactivity of COFs. Therefore, combining D-TA COFs by in situ confining CDs with MIP is expected to further improve the PEC performance, in terms of TC detection.

Under this direction, in this work, a cathodic MIP-PEC sensor for TC detection was developed based on in situ and fast fabrication of CDs/COFs heterojunction film. The proposed synthesis method of CDs/COFs is simple, fast, and convenient, and the introduction of CDs in the pore of COFs can effectively enhance the light absorption and electron conductivity of CDs/COFs. Furthermore, the selectivity and stability of the sensing interface were achieved with MIP using the rapid in situ UV-photopolymerization technology. As a result, the MIP-PEC sensor is capable of a wide linear range, highly sensitive, and stable TC detection due to the “turn off” large cathode photocurrent signal (Scheme 1 ). This work provides an effective method to construct uniform stable selective PEC sensing for antibiotics detection in the environment.
A comprehensive list of all reagents, materials, and instruments is provided in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ synthesis of CDs/COFs on ITO
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/COFs were modified onto the ITO via a rapid in-situ growth approach at room temperature, with the detailed procedural steps provided in the Supporting Information.

To improve the selectivity and robustness of the PEC sensor, MIP was prepared on the electrode surface by in situ UV photopolymerization (Mao et al., 2019), with the detailed procedural steps provided in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of the CDs/COFs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CDs was characterized by performing transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements, which was observed to be spherical 0D quantum dots with consistent size and an average diameter of 1.67 nm (Fig. 1 A). The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of CDs/COFs is shown in Fig. 1B. The CDs can be clearly observed in the uniform film-like structure of COFs. The lattice spacing of 0.34 nm corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of CDs can be clearly observed by further amplification in Fig. 1C (Zhang et al., 2019b). The above information confirmed the successful fabrication of 2D COFs confining 0D CDs film heterojunction.

Furthermore, the surface and cross section morphologies of ITO, COFs/ITO, CDs/COFs/ITO, and MIP/CDs/COFs/ITO were characterized by conducting scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements. The bare ITO surface exhibited a regular bulk crystal structure (Fig. 1D) with a thickness of 250 nm (Fig. 1G). COFs/ITO exhibited a relatively uniform, smooth film morphology of COFs (Fig. 1E), and a thickness of 24 nm on ITO (Fig. 1H). With the introduction of CDs, the granularity and thickness of the CDs/COFs film were increased slightly (Fig. 1F and I), which will be more conducive to the further adhesion of MIP. These morphological results demonstrated that CDs/COFs have a uniform nanoscale 2D film structure and are attached to the electrode surface tightly. The introduction of 0D CDs slightly changed the morphology of COFs. Elemental mapping and content analysis showed that the C, N, O elements were distributed in COFs uniformly (Fig. S1A). Following the introduction of CDs, which bear a profusion of surface amino groups, the normalized mass of C escalated from 18.18% to 21.76%, accompanied by an increase in the N content from 19.38% to 21.40% (Fig. S1B), which was conducive to improving the conductivity of COFs.

XRD measurements were conducted to elucidate the crystalline structure of the composite (Fig. 1J). The XRD pattern of pure COFs showed multiple diffraction peaks, indicating good crystallinity. The weak reflections and a broad peak at ∼25° 2θ, assigned to the (001) facet, confirmed the formation of 2D COFs in a crystalline and π–π stacked form (Pachfule et al., 2019). The CDs/COFs exhibited similar XRD patterns, suggesting that the COFs' crystalline structure was retained after incorporating CDs. The FT-IR indicated the presence of a Schiff base structure in COFs, formed by aldehyde-amine condensation (Fig. 1K). In particular, the C=N peak at 1620 cm−1 confirmed the successful synthesis of the imine structure (Chandra et al., 2013). After introducing CDs, the characteristic peak intensity near 1000 cm−1 for COFs slightly changed, suggesting a minor change in surface C–N functional groups. In the XPS of COFs, the characteristic peaks for C, N, and O elements were observed at 284.8 eV, 400 eV, and 532 eV, respectively. After introducing CDs, the C element peak intensity increased, indicating a higher C content at the electrode interface (Fig. 1L). High-resolution C1s spectra revealed C=N, C=C, and C=O bonds in both COFs and CDs/COFs (Fig. S2A and Fig. S2C), while the N1s spectra showed C=N, C–N, and –NH bonds (Fig. S2B and Fig. S2D) (Disha et al., 2022). The binding energies of C=N and C=O peaks in CDs/COFs showed only slight changes compared to COFs, suggesting the existence of potential bonding interactions between COFs and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC performance and enhancement mechanism of CDs/COFs
ce:section.ce:para:
The PEC signals of COFs versus CDs/COFs are shown in Fig. 2 A. The PEC signal of pure COFs was approximately −19 μA, and the introduction of CDs amplified the signal by a factor of 1.6 times. To further explore the mechanism of signal amplification, UV–vis diffuse reflectance (DRS) and fluorescence (FL) measurements were carried out. According to the literature, the characteristic absorption peaks within the visible spectrum have been attributed to the presence of various polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (Fu et al., 2015). More specifically, this involves the π–π∗ transitions of the C=C bonds and the n–π∗ transitions of the C=O groups (Bramhaiah et al., 2021; Wang and Hu, 2014). As can be observed, CDs have a strong absorption at 300–400 nm and a certain intensity of fluorescence emission at 430 nm (Fig. S3A, Fig. S3B). In parallel, the addition of CDs enhances the light absorption of COFs (Fig. 2B), which facilitates an enhanced separation of photogenerated charge carriers. In addition, the fluorescence characterization of COFs and CDs/COFs is also in direct agreement with this assumption. Generally, the recombination of photogenerated carriers emits strong fluorescence. Under the excitation of light with a wavelength of 300 nm, the fluorescence peak of CDs/COFs was significantly quenched compared with that of COFs at 690 nm (Fig. 2C). In the case of strong interactions between COFs and CDs, charge transfer at the composite interface may occur, suppressing the recombination of photogenerated carriers (Feijoo et al., 2024). Additionally, Fig. 2D demonstrates that CDs/COFs exhibit a higher incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) (Zhou et al., 2022). Furthermore, the open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) test (Fig. S4) indicates that CDs/COFs have a larger transient open-circuit voltage difference (ΔV oc) compared to COFs, suggesting more effective interfacial charge transfer under light excitation (Dashtian et al., 2020). The average photogenerated electron-hole lifetime (τn), calculated using the SV equation, is longer for CDs/COFs (Fig. 2E). These results confirm that the introduction of CDs enhances the photoelectrochemical performance of COFs (Li et al., 2021; Dashtian et al., 2019). Furthermore, the estimation of energy levels for both CDs and COFs is crucial for understanding the underlying electron transfer dynamics. First, the energy levels of CDs were determined by performing Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) scans. The initial reduction and oxidation potentials of CDs were recorded at −1.32 V and 1.20 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively (Fig. S5A and Fig. S5B), corresponding to the HOMO and LUMO energy levels. According to the Nernst equation, the ELUMO and EHOMO for CDs were calculated as −0.71 V and 1.81 V (vs. RHE), respectively. For COFs, Fig. S5C showed the initial oxidation potential (EOX) was 0.19 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), and the ELUMO was calculated as 0.80 V (vs. RHE). The energy gap (Eg) for COFs, based on the onset absorption wavelength (λ
onset ), was 1.74 V, calculated using Eg = 1240/λ
onset . The small band gap indicates higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The final ELUMO for COFs was calculated as −0.94 V using ELUMO = EHOMO - Eg. Additionally, the Mott-Schottky analysis corroborated these calculated results (Fig. S6). Therefore, CDs and COFs have matching energy levels, which can effectively improve the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. The CDs/COFs system forms a type II heterojunction (Fig. 2F). Under photoexcitation, the photogenerated electrons in the COFs conduction band are transferred to the CDs conduction band, while the photogenerated holes in the CDs valence band are transferred to the COFs valence band. This synergistic effect, combined with well-matched interfacial energy levels, facilitates the efficient transfer of photogenerated electrons to the solution, enhancing the carrier separation. As a result, the cathode photocurrent signal is amplified 1.6 times compared to that of single COFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and detection feasibility of the MIP-PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of MIP/CDs/COFs are shown in Fig. S7A. The presence of MIP on the surface of CDs/COFs was distinctly observable, with a thickness approximately measuring around 30 nm. Fig. S7B presents the elemental distribution on the electrode surface after MIP modification, demonstrating an increase in the normalized mass fraction of O from 56.84% to 64.85%, which substantiates the effective integration of the MIP.

Furthermore, to verify the feasibility of the MIP-PEC sensor for TC detection, the photocurrents of CDs/COFs, MIP/CDs/COFs (with template), r-MIP/CDs/COFs (template eluted, no target), and r-MIP/CDs/COFs exposed to various TC concentrations were measured (Fig. 3 A). CDs/COFs showed a strong photocurrent signal (−31 μA). After performing MIP modification, the photocurrent decreased to −5 μA due to poor conductivity and steric hindrance. Template elution restored the photocurrent to −31 μA, likely due to reduced steric hindrance and enhanced electrode interface stability (Chi and Liu, 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). Specific binding of TC to the imprinted cavity further decreased the photocurrent, confirming the feasibility of the sensor. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis (Fig. 3B) confirmed the successful MIP-PEC fabrication and detection. The high-frequency semicircle diameter reflects the electron transfer resistance (Ret), indicating the interface charge transfer efficiency (Liu et al., 2020). ITO showed a low Ret of 15 Ω, while CDs/COFs had a Ret of 285 Ω, indicating strong electron transfer. MIP modification increased Ret to 1567 Ω due to the poor conductivity and steric hindrance. Template elution decreased Ret to 644 Ω. Incubation with TC increased Ret to 950 Ω and 1233 Ω, proportional to TC concentration. These results confirm the successful sensor construction and the good TC detection capability.

To develop a highly sensitive MIP-PEC sensor for TC detection, various experimental conditions, such as CDs/COFs modified volume, MIP precursor solution modified volume, elution time, incubation time, and pH of PBS were optimized.

As shown in Fig. S8A, the maximum photocurrent signal was obtained with 13 μL of CDs/COFs. Lower volumes resulted in a non-uniform distribution on the ITO interface, while higher volumes thickened the layer and reduced the photocurrent values. The optimal baseline signal was achieved when the CDs concentration was 0.5 mg/mL (4 wt% of COFs), yielding a maximum photocurrent of −31 μA (Fig. S8B). Increasing the CDs loading beyond this point decreased the photocurrent, likely due to the aggregation effect that obstructed COFs pores and reduced electron mobility. Besides, the presence of excessive CDs that disrupt the COFs crystalline structure could explain the decrease in photocurrent (Feijoo et al., 2024).

In addition, the MIP layer thickness is affected by the volume of the MIP precursor solution (Karthika et al., 2021), while the maximum ΔI (difference in photocurrent between MIP and rMIP) was achieved with 8 μL of the MIP precursor solution (Fig. S9A). Smaller volumes resulted in fewer recognition sites, narrowing the detection range, while larger volumes led to thick layers, reducing active sites and sensitivity due to incomplete template extraction (Zhong et al., 2024). The introduction of MIP increased the electrode interface impedance but restored the photocurrent signal, likely enhancing the stability of the interface (Liu et al., 2015). The elution time was optimized to ensure that as many template molecules in the MIP were removed as possible (Fig. S9B). ΔI gradually increased with the extension of the elution time within 5–10 min and stabilized at 15 min. Then, it gradually decreased with the extension of the elution time, which may be due to the polar solvent causing damage to the MIP membrane and affecting the recognition ability (Ying et al., 2019). Target incubation time is also one of the important factors affecting ΔI, which determines whether the target can be captured by the MIP cavity to the greatest extent. As depicted in Fig. S9C, the photocurrent signal decreased with increasing the incubation time from 5 to 15 min, reaching equilibrium after 20 min. Thus, 20 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time. According to the literature, TC exists as a cation below pH 3.4, a neutral zwitterion between pH 3.4 and 7.7, and an anion above pH 9.7 (Aguilar et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2023). Additionally, in acidic or basic media, the -COOH functional group of MAA acquires a positive or negative charge, leading to electrostatic repulsion with TC, and thus affecting its recognition (Cao et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2024). The pH optimization (Fig. S9D) indicates that ΔI is maximized under neutral conditions, suggesting the strongest MIP recognition for TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and recognition mechanism of MIP
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimal conditions, the effective electroactive area of the MIP-PEC sensor was evaluated. By substituting the oxidation peak current values into the Randles-Sevcik equation, the effective electroactive area of the constructed MIP-PEC sensor was calculated to be 0.18 cm2 (Fig. 4 A). A larger electroactive area provides an efficient pathway for electron transfer, thereby enhancing the detection sensitivity of the sensor. Based on this approach, Fig. 4B shows the imprinting factor (IF) that was calculated as 6.52 (Li et al., 2024b; Dashtian et al., 2021b). The presence of TC-specific binding sites in MIP leads to a strong photocurrent response, resulting in higher sensitivity and selectivity compared to NIP sensors, which lack such imprinted cavities (Yoshikawa et al., 2016). Furthermore, based on the response values (ΔI) of the sensor to different concentrations of TC, the dissociation constants (Kd) were calculated using three different adsorption models (Langmuir, Freundlich, and Langmuir-Freundlich) to evaluate the binding affinity of the MIP film for TC (Fig. 4C) (He et al., 2024). The mathematical results are summarized in Table S1. The Langmuir-Freundlich model provided the best fit with an R
2 value of 0.993. This result suggests that the selective binding of TC molecules to the MIP film is governed by an interfacial adsorption process involving both homogeneous and heterogeneous adsorption (Sehit et al., 2020). Using the Langmuir-Freundlich model, the Kd value was calculated to be 0.38 nM, indicating a strong binding capacity between the MIP-PEC sensor and the TC molecules (Buffon and Stradiotto, 2019). The high affinity can be attributed to the formation of specific imprinted cavities, which enhance the selectivity of the sensor. To investigate the recognition mechanism between MIP and TC, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups and typical bonds before and after recognition (Wang et al., 2021b) (Fig. 4D). The following observations were made: A characteristic peak at 3400 cm⁻1, attributed to -OH groups on the TC surface, appeared and broadened with increasing TC concentration, shifting to lower wavenumbers. This effect suggests hydrogen bonding between the -OH groups of TC and the -COOH groups of methacrylic acid, reducing the vibration frequency of -OH (Diao et al., 2024). The peaks at 1718 cm⁻1 (-C=O) and 1148 cm⁻1 (-C-O-C-) of MIP were weakened and shifted, suggesting a potential hydrogen bonding between the -OH and -COOH groups (Zhang et al., 2022). The peak at 2950 cm⁻1, attributed to -CH₂- chains in MIP, broadened and shifted slightly to lower wavenumbers, indicating limited vibrational freedom of the MIP chains due to TC (Aguilar et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2024). These results indicate that TC binding is facilitated by non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding (Fig. 4E). The MIP film presented significant adsorption capacity and excellent recognition ability due to the complementary size, shape, and functional groups of the imprinted sites.

Under optimal conditions, the prepared MIP-PEC sensor was used to detect different concentrations of TC, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 A. As expected, the intensity of the photocurrent signal gradually decreased as the TC concentration increased from 1.00 × 10−12–5.00 × 10−5 M. As displayed in Fig. 5B, the calibration plot had a good linear relationship between the photocurrent decrease and the logarithm of the TC concentration (lg C TC) ranging from 5.00 × 10−12–1.00 × 10−5 M, fitted as I = 3.18 lg C TC - 23.30 with a correlation coefficient (R
2 ) of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 6.00 × 10−13 M using the International Union for Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) recommended method (Zhu et al., 2021). In addition, the detection performance of the proposed MIP-PEC sensor was compared with that of other MIP sensors that have been used for TC detection in recent years (Table S2). It can be also observed that the developed sensor has a wide linear range with relatively low LOD, which was attributed to the amplified cathode signal provided by CDs/COFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, reusability and stability of MIP-PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Assessing the selectivity of the MIP-PEC sensor, the photocurrent responses of rMIP/CDs/COFs/ITO were examined upon exposure to structurally similar antibiotics to TC, such as oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), sulfamethoxazole (SDM), ofloxacin (OFL) were recorded (Fig. 5C). The concentration of these distractors was 10 times higher than that of TC, and the photocurrent signal was reduced substantially only when TC (10 nM) was present, which indicates the excellent selectivity of the MIP-PEC sensor. The anti-interference performance of the sensor in the detection of TC in the actual water environment was also systematically investigated. When there were common K+, Ca2+, Na2+, Mg2+ ions or heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+ in the water, and the concentration was higher than 10 times of TC, the sensor can still maintain good precision performance for 10 nM TC (Fig. 5D). Thereby, it can be argued that the sensor can still maintain good reliability in the detection of harsh water samples. Subsequently, the reproducibility of the MIP-PEC sensor was investigated (Fig. 5E), and the photocurrent signals of eight parallel samples prepared under the same conditions were obtained. The relative standard deviation was calculated to be 1.77%, which demonstrated good reproducibility of the sensor. Moreover, the r-MIP-PEC sensor signal can still maintain 80% of the original signal after 4 consecutive incubation and elution cycles of TC (10 nM), and the sensor has good reusability (Fig. S10). Finally, a batch of MIP-PEC sensors was prepared under common conditions and stored at 4 °C. The photocurrent response signal of the sensor after TC identification was recorded for 14 consecutive days, as depicted in Fig. 5F. The RSD of the photocurrent signal of the MIP-PEC sensor was maintained at 1.36%. This indicates that the MIP-PEC sensor in this work has excellent long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of real water sample detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the practical application performance of the MIP-PEC sensor, the river water next to livestock was selected as the sample. Water samples underwent preliminary filtration to eliminate large particulates prior to PEC analysis. Table S3 illustrates that no TC was detected in the sampled river water, with findings corroborated by LC-MS/MS analysis. Then, to eliminate the interference of the matrix effect from real samples, the standard addition method was used to establish a standard curve using real water samples. According to the results, the photocurrent signal response of the MIP-PEC sensor was inversely proportional to lg C TC (Fig. S11A), and showed a good linear relationship, I = 2.79 lg C TC - 18.35, R
2 = 0.996 (Fig. S11B). In addition, compared with the standard curve established with ultrapure water, the sensitivity of the standard curve established with real samples was decreased. Nonetheless, it still exhibited excellent detection performance.

In addition, the detection performance of the developed sensor was investigated by the standard recovery approach. The MIP-PEC sensor demonstrated a recovery rate of 93–103% with an RSD of 1.48–2.92%, which was further substantiated by conducting parallel LC-MS/MS analysis. The high accuracy of the established technique for practical applications is also confirmed by the consistent outcomes between the MIP-PEC sensor and the reference methods.

In this work, a sensitive and selective MIP-PEC sensor for TC detection was established based on 0D-2D heterogeneous hybrid composites of CDs/COFs. CDs were encapsulated in the pore structure of COFs by simple aldehyde-amine condensation at room temperature, and the amplified cathodic photocurrent signal was obtained by enhancing light absorption and promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, the MIP-PEC sensor for selective TC detection was successfully constructed by the in-situ UV photopolymerization method on the electrode surface. The MIP-PEC sensor has excellent sensitivity and selectivity, with a linear range of 5.00 × 10−12–1.00 × 10−5 M. Finally, our work provides a novel and efficient construction method of MIP-PEC platform for the sensitive and selective detection of antibiotics or other pollutants in the environment.

Mengge Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiuxiu Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Guanya Ji: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jing Wang: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Tao Wang: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Qian Liu: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Qijian Niu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62101216, 62201230, 32301723), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220546), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671359, 2022T150275, 2020M671367), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD-2023-87), and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (SJCX24_2424).
